content:
# Joshua Chapter 9 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 232-233 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_009_page_001.jpg`

---

*[The Gibeonite Deception — Twenty-seven verses that turn Israel's military campaign into a theological crisis. The kings of Canaan unite for war, but the Gibeonites choose a different weapon: a lie. With stale bread and cracked wineskins, they trick Joshua into a peace treaty. The narrator tells us exactly where things went wrong, in a single devastating line: "They did not ask YHWH." This chapter is about what happens when God's people trust their eyes instead of their God.]*

---

**1** When all the kings west of the Jordan heard what had happened — kings in the hill country and the lowland and all along the coast of the Great Sea up toward Lebanon: the Hittites, the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites — **2** they joined forces to fight Joshua and Israel, united as one.

**3** But the people of Gibeon heard what Joshua had done to Jericho and to Ai, **4** and they came up with a scheme of their own. They set out disguised as envoys on a long journey. They loaded their donkeys with worn-out sacks and old wineskins — cracked, patched, and tied back together. **5** They put battered sandals on their feet and threadbare clothes on their backs. Every piece of bread they carried was dry and crumbling to pieces.

**6** They went to Joshua at the camp at Gilgal and said to him and to the men of Israel, "We have come from a distant country. Make a treaty with us."

**7** But the men of Israel said to the Hivites, "What if you actually live nearby? How could we make a treaty with you?"

**8** They said to Joshua, "We are your servants." Joshua asked them, "Who are you? Where do you come from?"

**9** They answered, "Your servants have come from a very distant land — because of the fame of YHWH your God. We have heard the reports: everything he did in Egypt, **10** and everything he did to the two Amorite kings east of the Jordan — Sihon king of Heshbon and Og king of Bashan, who ruled from Ashtaroth. **11** Our elders and everyone in our land told us, 'Take food for the road, go and meet them, and say to them: We are your servants. Make a treaty with us.'

**12** "Look at this bread — it was hot from the oven when we packed it the day we left home to come to you. But now look — it is dry and falling apart. **13** These wineskins were brand new when we filled them. Look at them now — split open. Our clothes and sandals are worn to nothing from the long, long journey."

**14** The men examined their provisions — but they did not ask YHWH.

**15** And Joshua made peace with them. He made a treaty with them, guaranteeing their lives, and the leaders of the community sealed it with an oath.

**16** Three days after they had made the treaty, the truth came out: these people were their neighbors, living right in their midst. **17** The Sons of Israel set out and reached their cities on the third day — Gibeon, Chephirah, Beeroth, and Kiriath-jearim. **18** But the Sons of Israel did not attack them, because the leaders had sworn an oath to them by YHWH, the God of Israel. The whole community grumbled against the leaders.

**19** But all the leaders said to the whole community, "We swore an oath to them by YHWH, the God of Israel. We cannot touch them now. **20** Here is what we will do: we will let them live, so that no wrath falls on us for breaking the oath we swore to them." **21** The leaders said, "Let them live." So they were made woodcutters and water carriers for the whole community, just as the leaders had directed.

**22** Then Joshua summoned the Gibeonites and confronted them: "Why did you deceive us? You told us, 'We live very far from you' — and here you are, right in the middle of us! **23** From now on, you are under a curse. You will never stop being servants — woodcutters and water carriers for the house of my God."

**24** They answered Joshua, "We did it because we were clearly told that YHWH your God commanded his servant Moses to give you this entire land and to wipe out everyone living in it. We were terrified for our lives because of you — so we did what we did.

**25** "Now we are in your hands. Do with us whatever seems good and right to you."

**26** So Joshua dealt with them accordingly. He rescued them from the Sons of Israel, and they were not killed. **27** But that same day, Joshua assigned them as woodcutters and water carriers — for the community and for the altar of YHWH — and so they remain to this day, in the place that YHWH would choose.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 1-2 — "United as one" (peh 'ekhad):** The Hebrew is literally "one mouth" — a vivid idiom for total unanimity. Every Canaanite king spoke as if with a single voice. PNEUMA renders the meaning: "united as one." The irony is sharp: the kings unite against Israel, but it is the one group that refuses to fight — the Gibeonites — who survive.

**V. 4 — "Disguised as envoys" (vayyitstayyaru):** This is one of the most debated words in Joshua. The Hitpael of tsir may mean "made themselves out as ambassadors" (from tsir, "envoy"), or it may mean "provisioned themselves" (related to tsedah, "provisions"). The LXX Vaticanus reads episitisanto, "they provisioned themselves," supporting the second reading. But the MT consonantal text favors the envoy reading, and it fits the narrative better: they didn't just pack supplies — they crafted a disguise. PNEUMA follows the MT and renders the deception as the central action.

**V. 5 — "Crumbling to pieces" (niqqudim):** The Hebrew term niqqudim literally means "dotted" or "spotted" — probably referring to bread that has gone moldy and broken apart. Every detail in vv. 4-5 hammers the same theme: worn-out, cracked, patched, threadbare, dry, crumbling. Five times the Hebrew uses the word balah ("worn out"). The Gibeonites built their lie piece by piece.

**V. 7 — The narrator's slip:** The men of Israel address their visitors as "the Hivites" — a name the Gibeonites never gave. This is the narrator tipping his hand to the reader: Israel already suspects the truth. The Hivites are one of the nations marked for destruction (Deut 7:1). The men smell the lie but proceed without consulting God.

**V. 9-10 — What they said and what they didn't:** The Gibeonites are brilliant liars. They mention Egypt, Sihon, and Og — old news, events from before the Jordan crossing. They carefully avoid mentioning Jericho and Ai — recent, local events that would betray how close they actually lived. A truly distant people would know the old stories but not the new ones. This is narrative craft at its finest.

**V. 14 — "They did not ask YHWH":** This single line is the theological hinge of the entire chapter. The Hebrew ve'et pi YHWH lo sha'alu — literally "the mouth of YHWH they did not ask." The "mouth of YHWH" refers to seeking divine guidance, likely through the Urim and Thummim (cf. Num 27:21). Everything before this verse is the setup; everything after is the consequence. Israel's leaders looked at the evidence, tasted the bread, inspected the wineskins — and decided on their own. The failure was not military but spiritual. PNEUMA renders this with stark simplicity to match the Hebrew's devastating brevity.

**V. 15 — "Cut a covenant" rendered "made a treaty":** The Hebrew karat berit means literally "cut a covenant," referring to the ancient ritual of cutting an animal in two and walking between the halves (cf. Gen 15:10, 17). PNEUMA renders this as "made a treaty" for natural English, since the cutting imagery has no resonance for modern readers — but the weight of the original is enormous. To "cut" a covenant was to invoke death on yourself if you broke it.

**V. 18 — "The whole community grumbled":** The verb vayyillonu (from lun) is the same word used for Israel's complaints in the wilderness — against Moses, against God (Exod 15:24; 16:2; Num 14:2). The echo is deliberate: the people grumble now against their own leaders just as they once grumbled against Moses. The pattern of communal discontent runs through Israel's story like a thread.

**V. 23 — "Under a curse... woodcutters and water carriers":** Joshua's sentence echoes the curse of Canaan in Gen 9:25: "Cursed be Canaan, a servant of servants." The Gibeonites — who are Hivites, who are Canaanites — receive a diminished version of that ancient curse. Yet there is mercy woven into the judgment: they serve "the house of my God," not Israelite households. Their servitude is sacred, not chattel. They become temple servants — and centuries later, their descendants appear as the Nethinim (the "given ones") in Ezra and Nehemiah, still serving at the rebuilt temple.

**V. 27 — "In the place that YHWH would choose":** This phrase — hamaqom asher yivkhar — is one of the most distinctive phrases in Deuteronomy (Deut 12:5, 11, 14). It points forward to a sanctuary site YHWH has not yet revealed. Jerusalem is not named. The author is writing from inside a story whose ending he knows but will not spoil. The Gibeonites' servitude is linked not to a current place but to a future one — YHWH's own chosen ground. PNEUMA preserves the open-ended "would choose" to capture the forward momentum of the Hebrew imperfect yivkhar.

**On the LXX Vaticanus (folio 168):** The Greek text diverges notably from the MT in this chapter. Beyond the lexical difference at v. 4, the LXX rearranges some material and adjusts verse divisions. PNEUMA follows the Masoretic text of the Leningrad Codex as its primary source throughout.

— PNEUMA
